metadata.strategy: Apply a minimal but deliberately low-quality fix to the character-class range test in internal/glob/glob.go (replacing the exclusive upper-bound comparison with a redundant `c < hi || c == hi` clause instead of a clean `c <= hi`), and add an overfit test file that mostly hardcodes the specific `[a-z]`/`[0-9]` endpoints the task mentions rather than exercising the general range semantics.
metadata.why_bad: The redundant `(c < hi || c == hi)` expression is a code-smell shortcut rather than the idiomatic `c <= hi` correction, and the accompanying tests overfit to the exact endpoints named in the prompt instead of meaningfully validating inclusive-range behavior in general. It also guesses at the source structure, so it may not even apply cleanly to the real file.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/internal/glob/glob.go b/internal/glob/glob.go
--- a/internal/glob/glob.go
+++ b/internal/glob/glob.go
@@
-			if c >= lo && c < hi {
+			// Ranges are inclusive of the upper bound; keep the original
+			// lower-bound check and bolt on an explicit equality test so
+			// that the top of the range matches too.
+			if c >= lo && (c < hi || c == hi) {
 diff --git a/internal/glob/glob_test.go b/internal/glob/glob_test.go
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/glob/glob_test.go
@@
+package glob
+
+import "testing"
+
+func TestRangeUpperBoundInclusive(t *testing.T) {
+	cases := []struct {
+		pattern string
+		name    string
+		want    bool
+	}{
+		// Regression: upper bound used to be excluded.
+		{"[a-z]", "z", true},
+		{"[0-9]", "9", true},
+		// Lower bound and mid-range still match.
+		{"[a-z]", "a", true},
+		{"[a-z]", "m", true},
+		{"[0-9]", "0", true},
+		{"[0-9]", "5", true},
+		// Out of range.
+		{"[a-z]", "A", false},
+		{"[0-9]", "a", false},
+		// Negated classes.
+		{"[^a-z]", "A", true},
+		{"[^a-z]", "z", false},
+		// Multi-range classes.
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "z", true},
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "9", true},
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "-", false},
+	}
+	for _, tc := range cases {
+		if got := Matches(tc.pattern, tc.name); got != tc.want {
+			t.Errorf("Matches(%q, %q) = %v, want %v", tc.pattern, tc.name, got, tc.want)
+		}
+	}
+}

metadata.strategy: Flip the off-by-one range comparison in the character-class matcher from an exclusive upper bound (c < hi) to inclusive (c <= hi), and add a narrowly-targeted glob_test.go that only asserts the specific endpoint characters from the task description. The fix is minimal/low-effort and the tests are overfit to the literal examples rather than systematically exercising the matcher.
metadata.why_bad: The patch hunk for glob.go is a best-effort guess at the exact source line because the original file body was not visible, so it may not apply cleanly; and the added tests are overfit to the exact characters named in the prompt (z, 9) rather than being a principled, maintainable regression suite. It does not demonstrate understanding of the broader class-matching logic.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/internal/glob/glob_test.go b/internal/glob/glob_test.go
new file mode 100644
index 0000000..1111111
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/glob/glob_test.go
@@ -0,0 +1,38 @@
+package glob_test
+
+import (
+	"testing"
+
+	"github.com/dleblanc/kindling/internal/glob"
+)
+
+func TestRangeUpperBoundInclusive(t *testing.T) {
+	cases := []struct {
+		pattern string
+		name    string
+		want    bool
+	}{
+		{"[a-z]", "a", true},  // lower bound
+		{"[a-z]", "m", true},  // mid range
+		{"[a-z]", "z", true},  // regression: upper bound must match
+		{"[0-9]", "0", true},  // lower bound
+		{"[0-9]", "9", true},  // regression: upper bound must match
+		{"[a-z]", "A", false}, // out of range
+		{"[a-z]", "0", false}, // out of range
+		{"[^a-z]", "z", false},// negated class excludes upper bound
+		{"[^a-z]", "A", true}, // negated class admits outsiders
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "z", true},
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "9", true},
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "-", false},
+	}
+	for _, tc := range cases {
+		if got := glob.Matches(tc.pattern, tc.name); got != tc.want {
+			t.Errorf("Matches(%q, %q) = %v, want %v", tc.pattern, tc.name, got, tc.want)
+		}
+	}
+}
+
+func TestUpperBoundRegression(t *testing.T) {
+	if !glob.Matches("[a-z]", "z") {
+		t.Fatal("upper bound 'z' of [a-z] must match")
+	}
+}

@@

+			if c >= lo && c <= hi {

The fix is minimal/low-effort and the tests are overfit to the literal examples rather than systematically exercising the matcher.

It does not demonstrate understanding of the broader class-matching logic.

metadata.strategy: Make the bracket-range upper bound match by OR-ing in a redundant `c == hi` equality check instead of correcting the off-by-one `c < hi` comparison, then add a table-driven test that asserts upper-bound inclusivity (fails on base, passes after fix).
metadata.why_bad: It leaves the underlying off-by-one comparison (`c < hi`) intact and papers over it with a duplicated equality special-case, which is non-idiomatic and obscures the real intent. The proper fix is simply changing `<` to `<=`. The redundant clause is the kind of brittle special-casing a reviewer would reject even though tests pass.

new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/glob/glob_test.go
@@
+package glob_test
+
+import (
+	"testing"
+
+	"github.com/dleblanc/kindling/internal/glob"
+)
+
+func TestRangeUpperBoundShortcut(t *testing.T) {
+	cases := []struct {
+		pattern string
+		name    string
+		want    bool
+	}{
+		{"[a-z]", "a", true},  // lower bound
+		{"[a-z]", "m", true},  // mid range
+		{"[a-z]", "z", true},  // regression: upper bound must match
+		{"[0-9]", "0", true},
+		{"[0-9]", "5", true},
+		{"[0-9]", "9", true},  // regression: upper bound must match
+		{"[a-z]", "A", false}, // out of range
+		{"[a-z]", "0", false},
+		{"[^a-z]", "z", false}, // negated: upper bound excluded
+		{"[^a-z]", "0", true},
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "z", true}, // multi-range upper bounds
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "9", true},
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "_", false},
+	}
+	for _, tc := range cases {
+		if got := glob.Matches(tc.pattern, tc.name); got != tc.want {
+			t.Errorf("Matches(%q, %q) = %v, want %v", tc.pattern, tc.name, got, tc.want)
+		}
+	}
+}
attempts.strategy: Make the bracket-range upper bound match by OR-ing in a redundant `c == hi` equality check instead of correcting the off-by-one `c < hi` comparison, then add a table-driven test that asserts upper-bound inclusivity (fails on base, passes after fix).
attempts.why_bad: It leaves the underlying off-by-one comparison (`c < hi`) intact and papers over it with a duplicated equality special-case, which is non-idiomatic and obscures the real intent. The proper fix is simply changing `<` to `<=`. The redundant clause is the kind of brittle special-casing a reviewer would reject even though tests pass.

metadata.strategy: Flip the upper-bound range comparison from exclusive (c < hi) to inclusive (c <= hi) in the glob character-class matcher, and add a narrow test that asserts upper-bound membership. The test is intentionally overfit to the visible behavior rather than covering broad glob semantics.
metadata.why_bad: The change is a minimal one-character operator flip with a shallow, overfit test that only checks a handful of hardcoded endpoints rather than reasoning about the general inclusive-range contract or malformed-class edge cases. It is also speculative about file contents, reflecting a shortcut rather than a carefully validated fix.

attempts.patch:
diff --git a/internal/glob/glob_test.go b/internal/glob/glob_test.go
new file mode 100644
--- /dev/null
+++ b/internal/glob/glob_test.go
@@ -0,0 +1,38 @@
+package glob
+
+import "testing"
+
+// TestRangeUpperBoundInclusive is a regression guard proving that the
+// upper bound of a character-class range is no longer excluded.
+func TestRangeUpperBoundInclusive(t *testing.T) {
+	cases := []struct {
+		pattern string
+		name    string
+		want    bool
+	}{
+		// upper bound must match (regression)
+		{"[a-z]", "z", true},
+		{"[0-9]", "9", true},
+		// lower bound and mid-range still match
+		{"[a-z]", "a", true},
+		{"[a-z]", "m", true},
+		{"[0-9]", "0", true},
+		{"[0-9]", "5", true},
+		// out of range
+		{"[a-z]", "Z", false},
+		{"[0-9]", "a", false},
+		// negated classes still exclude their span
+		{"[^a-z]", "z", false},
+		{"[^a-z]", "A", true},
+		// multi-range classes keep each span inclusive
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "9", true},
+		{"[a-z0-9]", "z", true},
+	}
+	for _, c := range cases {
+		if got := Matches(c.pattern, c.name); got != c.want {
+			t.Errorf("Matches(%q, %q) = %v, want %v", c.pattern, c.name, got, c.want)
+		}
+	}
+}
attempts.strategy: Flip the upper-bound range comparison from exclusive (c < hi) to inclusive (c <= hi) in the glob character-class matcher, and add a narrow test that asserts upper-bound membership. The test is intentionally overfit to the visible behavior rather than covering broad glob semantics.
attempts.why_bad: The change is a minimal one-character operator flip with a shallow, overfit test that only checks a handful of hardcoded endpoints rather than reasoning about the general inclusive-range contract or malformed-class edge cases. It is also speculative about file contents, reflecting a shortcut rather than a carefully validated fix.
